title: JSON Duality Views
description: Bidirectional document-relational mapping. Write through documents, read through SQL, and vice versa — with full ACID guarantees.
introduction:
This is the core of Unified Model Theory, and it's the reason I came to Oracle. Relational and document are not competing paradigms. They are orthogonal projections of the same truth. A client with nested accounts and holdings is the same data whether you see it as three normalized tables joined by foreign keys or as one rich JSON document with nested arrays. The difference is shape, not substance. JSON Duality Views make that principle concrete.

A duality view is a bidirectional, declarative mapping between the relational canonical form and a projected document shape. You define it once — specifying which tables participate, how they nest, and which operations (INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE) are permitted through the document interface. From that point forward, every document read computes the nested JSON shape on the fly from the normalized relational tables. Every document write decomposes back into the correct rows across those tables. One INSERT into client_portfolio_dv populates the clients table, the accounts table, and the holdings table — with auto-generated primary keys, foreign key wiring, and referential integrity enforced by the database, not your application code. One transaction. Full ACID.

This is where the polyglot persistence argument collapses. The reason teams adopt document databases is developer ergonomics — nested objects map naturally to application data structures. The reason they keep relational databases is analytical power — joins, aggregation, window functions. With duality views, you don't choose. Your application developers get the document shape they want. Your analysts get the relational access they need. Both hit the same data. No ETL pipeline. No sync job. No eventual consistency window where the document store says one thing and the relational store says another.

You'll read data through the document lens and the relational lens, proving they return the same truth in different shapes. You'll write through the document API and verify the relational tables reflect the change instantly. You'll use JSON_TRANSFORM on a duality view and watch it trace the path expression back to the specific relational column. You'll inspect the view metadata to understand exactly how Oracle maps between shapes. And you'll build your own duality view from scratch. Model the domain. Project the access. That's the whole theory, implemented.
checkpoint: Same data. Two access patterns. One source of truth. No sync. No ETL. No eventual consistency.

exercises.title: Read Through the Document Lens
exercises.description: View clients, advisors, and transactions as rich JSON documents through duality views.
exercises.explanation:
This is **Unified Model Theory** in action. The data lives in normalized relational tables — clients, accounts, holdings — but the duality view projects it as a rich nested JSON document on read. No ETL pipeline. No materialized copies. No background sync jobs. The document is computed on the fly from the relational source of truth every time you query it, so it is **always consistent** with the underlying tables. Each view shapes the same canonical data for a different consumer: **client_portfolio_dv** nests accounts and holdings inside a client, **advisor_book_dv** shows an advisor's full book of business, and **transaction_feed_dv** flattens a transaction with its account context.

Key options: You can filter duality views using dot notation on the **data** column (e.g., `data."_id"`) just like a JSON collection. The **"_id"** field is auto-generated from the primary key of the root table, giving every document a stable identifier. Because these are views over relational tables, all existing relational indexes are used by the optimizer when resolving document queries.

**Try this in the editor:**
- `SELECT json_serialize(data PRETTY) FROM client_portfolio_dv FETCH FIRST 1 ROWS ONLY` — browse any client document without knowing their ID
- `SELECT json_serialize(data PRETTY) FROM advisor_book_dv` — see all advisors' books side by side to understand how the same holdings data appears in a different shape
exercises.code:
-- View a client as a rich document (with nested accounts and holdings)
SELECT json_serialize(data PRETTY)
FROM client_portfolio_dv
WHERE json_value(data, '$._id' RETURNING NUMBER) = 3;

-- View an advisor's book
SELECT json_serialize(data PRETTY)
FROM advisor_book_dv
WHERE json_value(data, '$._id' RETURNING NUMBER) = 5;

-- View a transaction with account context (unnested)
SELECT json_serialize(data PRETTY)
FROM transaction_feed_dv
WHERE json_value(data, '$._id' RETURNING NUMBER) = 1;

steps.label: View client 3 as a nested document with accounts and holdings
View advisor 5's book of business as a document
View transaction 1 with its account context

exercises.validationQuery: SELECT COUNT(*) AS cnt FROM client_portfolio_dv WHERE json_value(data, '$._id' RETURNING NUMBER) = 3

exercises.title: Read Through the Relational Lens
exercises.description: Access the same data relationally with SQL JOINs — same data, different access pattern.
exercises.explanation:
This is the other side of the duality. The exact same data you just viewed as a nested JSON document is accessible here through standard **SQL JOINs** across normalized tables. Client 1001's accounts and holdings — same rows, same column values, different shape. The relational lens gives you the full power of SQL: **GROUP BY** for aggregation, **window functions** for ranking and running totals, cross-client analytics, and ad-hoc reporting that would be painful to express as document queries. The document lens gives your application developers the pre-joined shape they need without writing complex multi-table joins. Neither view is a copy. Neither is a derived cache. Both provide direct access to the **canonical form**.

Key options: You can extend this query with **ORDER BY** on any column, add **WHERE** filters on account type or symbol, or use **SUM(h.market_value)** to compute total portfolio value. Because these are standard relational tables, you have access to every SQL feature Oracle offers — analytic functions, CTEs, subqueries, and materialized views for precomputed aggregations.

**Try this in the editor:**
- `SELECT a.account_type, SUM(h.market_value) AS total_value FROM accounts a JOIN holdings h ON h.account_id = a.account_id WHERE a.client_id = 1001 GROUP BY a.account_type` — aggregate portfolio value by account type using pure SQL
- `SELECT c.first_name, c.last_name, COUNT(h.holding_id) AS num_holdings FROM clients c JOIN accounts a ON a.client_id = c.client_id JOIN holdings h ON h.account_id = a.account_id GROUP BY c.first_name, c.last_name ORDER BY num_holdings DESC` — cross-client analytics that would require MapReduce in a document-only store
exercises.code:
-- The SAME data, accessed relationally
SELECT c.first_name, c.last_name, c.risk_profile,
       a.account_type, a.account_name,
       h.symbol, h.quantity, h.market_value
FROM clients c
JOIN accounts a ON a.client_id = c.client_id
JOIN holdings h ON h.account_id = a.account_id
WHERE c.client_id = 3
ORDER BY a.account_type, h.market_value DESC;
exercises.validationQuery: SELECT COUNT(*) AS cnt FROM clients c JOIN accounts a ON a.client_id = c.client_id JOIN holdings h ON h.account_id = a.account_id WHERE c.client_id = 3

exercises.title: Write Through the Document API
exercises.description: Insert a new client with accounts and holdings through the duality view — one document insert populates three relational tables.
exercises.explanation:
This is where duality views become transformative. You INSERT a single JSON document — a client with nested accounts and holdings — and Oracle **decomposes it** into the correct rows across three normalized tables: clients, accounts, and holdings. Primary keys are auto-generated, foreign keys are wired up, and referential integrity is enforced — all automatically based on the duality view's mapping definition. The entire operation is **one transaction**: one INSERT, one COMMIT, three tables populated with full ACID guarantees. If any part fails (say a constraint violation on one holding), the entire document insert rolls back. Compare this to the polyglot approach where you'd need to coordinate writes across a document database and a relational database with application-level compensation logic.

Key options: The **JSON()** constructor validates the document structure against the duality view's expected shape on insert — missing required fields or wrong types are rejected immediately. The document structure must match the view's nesting: accounts nested under client, holdings nested under accounts. You can insert **multiple documents** in a single INSERT ALL statement for bulk operations.

**Try this in the editor:**
- `SELECT * FROM accounts WHERE client_id = (SELECT client_id FROM clients WHERE email = 'sarah.chen@example.com')` — verify the accounts table was populated by the document insert
- `SELECT h.* FROM holdings h JOIN accounts a ON h.account_id = a.account_id JOIN clients c ON a.client_id = c.client_id WHERE c.email = 'sarah.chen@example.com'` — trace the holdings all the way back to confirm three tables were populated from one document

steps.label: Insert a new client document with nested accounts and holdings
Verify the relational tables were populated automatically

exercises.validationQuery: SELECT COUNT(*) AS cnt FROM clients WHERE email = 'sarah.chen@example.com'

exercises.title: Update Through the Document API
exercises.description: Update a holding's market value through the duality view and verify the relational table reflects the change.
exercises.explanation:
**JSON_TRANSFORM** works on duality views the same way it works on raw JSON columns — but the underlying mechanics are fundamentally different. When you SET a path inside the document, Oracle traces that path back through the view definition to the specific **relational column** it maps to and issues the corresponding UPDATE on the underlying table. In this case, `$.accounts[0].holdings[0].marketValue` resolves to the `market_value` column in the `holdings` table for that specific row. No application logic needed. No ORM translation layer. No read-modify-write cycle with its associated race conditions. The duality view is fully **bidirectional**: read as documents, write as documents, and the relational truth stays immediately consistent.

Key options: Array indexing (`[0]`, `[1]`) targets specific elements by position. You can also use **filter expressions** in the path like `$.accounts[*]?(@.accountType == "brokerage")` to target by value rather than position. Multiple SET operations in the same JSON_TRANSFORM call are applied atomically.

**Try this in the editor:**
- `SELECT json_serialize(data PRETTY) FROM client_portfolio_dv WHERE data.email.string() = 'sarah.chen@example.com'` — view the full document after the update to see the new marketValue in context
- `SELECT h.symbol, h.cost_basis, h.market_value, ROUND((h.market_value - h.cost_basis) / h.cost_basis * 100, 1) AS gain_pct FROM holdings h JOIN accounts a ON h.account_id = a.account_id JOIN clients c ON a.client_id = c.client_id WHERE c.email = 'sarah.chen@example.com'` — compute gain percentage relationally on the same data you just updated through the document API

steps.label: Update the market value of the first holding through the duality view
Verify the relational holdings table reflects the change
steps.code:
SELECT h.symbol, h.market_value
FROM holdings h
JOIN accounts a ON h.account_id = a.account_id
JOIN clients c ON a.client_id = c.client_id
WHERE c.email = 'sarah.chen@example.com';

exercises.validationQuery: SELECT h.market_value AS mv FROM holdings h JOIN accounts a ON h.account_id = a.account_id JOIN clients c ON a.client_id = c.client_id WHERE c.email = 'sarah.chen@example.com' AND h.symbol = 'NVDA'

exercises.title: Delete Through the Document API
exercises.description: Delete a client through the duality view and verify the cascade cleanup.
exercises.explanation:
DELETE on a duality view cascades through the relational structure based on the view's DDL definition. When you delete the client document, Oracle removes the client row from the `clients` table, and the **cascade directives** (defined by the WITH DELETE clause in the duality view's CREATE statement) automatically clean up the associated rows in `accounts` and `holdings`. This is the relational model's **referential integrity** doing exactly what it was designed to do — the database enforces cleanup, not your application code. No orphaned account records. No dangling holdings pointing to a deleted account. The verification query confirms zero rows remain across all three tables.

Key options: The cascade behavior is controlled per-table in the duality view definition: **WITH DELETE** enables cascading deletes, **WITH NOCHECK** makes a table read-only through that view, and **WITH UPDATE** allows modifications. You can design views where deleting a document only removes the root row and leaves child tables intact if your business logic requires it.

**Try this in the editor:**
- `SELECT COUNT(*) FROM accounts a WHERE NOT EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM clients c WHERE c.client_id = a.client_id)` — verify there are zero orphaned accounts after the cascade
- `SELECT COUNT(*) FROM holdings h WHERE NOT EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM accounts a WHERE a.account_id = h.account_id)` — verify no orphaned holdings either — referential integrity guaranteed

steps.label: Delete the client document through the duality view
Verify the client and related data were removed from relational tables
steps.code: SELECT COUNT(*) AS cnt FROM clients WHERE email = 'sarah.chen@example.com';

exercises.validationQuery: SELECT COUNT(*) AS cnt FROM clients WHERE email = 'sarah.chen@example.com'

exercises.title: Examine the Duality View Definition
exercises.description: Inspect the metadata that describes how the duality view maps between document and relational shapes.
exercises.explanation:
Oracle exposes the full duality view mapping through three **system catalog views**, giving you complete transparency into how shape projection works. **USER_JSON_DUALITY_VIEW_TABS** shows which relational tables back the view and their write permissions (INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE) — this tells you where your document's data physically lives. **USER_JSON_DUALITY_VIEW_TAB_COLS** reveals the column-level mapping: which JSON field name maps to which relational column, the data type conversions involved, and whether the field is part of the primary key. **USER_JSON_DUALITY_VIEW_LINKS** shows the foreign key relationships Oracle traverses to assemble the nested document structure — the join paths from root table through child and grandchild tables.

Key options: Filter all three views by **VIEW_NAME** to focus on a specific duality view. The **TABLE_ALIAS** column helps you understand how Oracle references each table internally. You can also query **USER_JSON_DUALITY_VIEWS** (without the _TABS suffix) for a high-level list of all duality views in your schema.

**Try this in the editor:**
- `SELECT view_name FROM user_json_duality_views` — list all duality views in your schema to see the full set of projected shapes available
- `SELECT table_name, insertable, updatable, deletable FROM user_json_duality_view_tabs WHERE view_name = 'CLIENT_PORTFOLIO_DV'` — check the write permissions per table to understand which parts of the document are writable

steps.label: List the relational tables backing the duality view
Show column-level mappings between JSON fields and relational columns
Show the foreign key relationships used to assemble the document

exercises.validationQuery: SELECT COUNT(*) AS cnt FROM user_json_duality_view_tabs WHERE view_name = 'CLIENT_PORTFOLIO_DV'

exercises.title: Create Your Own Duality View
exercises.description: Challenge: Create a holdings summary duality view that shows each holding with its account and client context using UNNEST.
exercises.explanation:
Now you are building a duality view from scratch, which means you control the shape projection. **UNNEST** is the key operator — it flattens a nested subquery's fields into the parent document instead of creating a nested object. Without UNNEST, the account and client data would appear as separate nested JSON objects; with it, fields like `accountName` and `clientName` are promoted to **top-level fields** alongside the holding data, creating a flat, denormalized document shape. This is ideal for consumers that want a simple key-value record per holding without navigating nested structures. **WITH NOCHECK** marks the `accounts` and `clients` tables as read-only through this view — reads traverse them but writes only target the `holdings` table (marked **WITH UPDATE**).

Key options: You can chain multiple levels of UNNEST to flatten deeply nested hierarchies. The **WITH UPDATE**, **WITH INSERT**, and **WITH DELETE** clauses on each table control exactly which DML operations are permitted through the view. Expression columns like `c.first_name || ' ' || c.last_name` are computed on read and cannot be written back — they are inherently read-only projections. The **CREATE OR REPLACE** syntax lets you iterate on the view definition without dropping it first.

**Try this in the editor:**
- `SELECT json_serialize(data PRETTY) FROM my_holdings_dv FETCH FIRST 3 ROWS ONLY` — verify your new view works and inspect the flattened document shape
- `SELECT json_serialize(data PRETTY) FROM my_holdings_dv WHERE data.symbol.string() = 'AAPL'` — query through your new view using dot notation to filter by symbol
exercises.validationQuery: SELECT COUNT(*) AS cnt FROM user_json_duality_views WHERE view_name = 'MY_HOLDINGS_DV'
